stories.title: 557K-SF Lakeland Warehouse Facility Trades for $67.7M
stories.excerpt: MDH paid $67.7 million for the Lakeland Commer Center Buildings 100-300 in Lakeland, Florida. The three-building portfolio totals 557,000 square feet of space. Stonemount was the seller. A JLL Capital Markets team representing Stonemount was led by Cody Brais, John Huguenard, Luis Castillo, Taylor Osborne, David Orta Jr. and Mia Gian. Lakeland Commerce Center is situated on 28.5 acres at 3490 County Line Road in Polk County. The properties are located within a master-planned business park positioned along the County Line Road corridor between Interstate 4 and Lakeland Linder International Airport. Lakeland is between Tampa and Orlando. Lakeland’s industrial market recorded nearly 3 million square feet of net absorption in 2025, matching its historic 2021 peak performance and marking a 74% increase year-over-year. The market maintains more than 10 million square feet of active tenant requirements, with leasing activity concentrated in spaces under 50,000 square feet, which represented more than 95% of transactions executed in 2025. The post 557K-SF Lakeland Warehouse Facility Trades for $67.7M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Lakeland, FL industrial market absorbed 3M SF in 2025, matching 2021 peak; 74% YoY growth
MDH acquired 557K SF Lakeland Commerce Center for $67.7M; 28.5-acre business park near I-4
95% of Lakeland leasing activity in spaces under 50K SF; 10M+ SF active tenant requirements
stories.summary: MDH paid $67.7 million for the 557,000-square-foot Lakeland Commerce Center in Polk County, Florida. Lakeland's industrial market delivered near-historic absorption in 2025 with 3 million square feet and strong forward demand signals (10M+ SF in active requirements). The majority of leasing activity concentrates in smaller spaces under 50,000 square feet.
stories.impact: Lakeland's industrial strength reflects regional logistics and distribution demand tied to Tampa/Orlando growth corridors. Strong absorption and tenant requirements signal underlying population and economic expansion—multifamily developers should monitor job creation in the I-4 corridor and warehouse/logistics wage growth as indicators of rental demand migration into Lakeland and surrounding Polk County submarkets.

stories.title: Raleigh Developer to Start Work on 269-Unit Rental Community
stories.excerpt: Blue Heron Asset Management and design firm Cline will begin work soon on a 269-unit apartment project in Raleigh. The project, called Rosewood, will be at 611 West South Street. Blue Heron completed the $12.85 million land acquisition earlier this year. Spanning 399,000 square feet across two buildings and a central public plaza, Rosewood will comprise units ranging from 499 to 1,531 square feet and will offer flexible indoor and outdoor resident spaces. Amenity highlights include a custom sauna and cold plunge suite, private coworking areas, and a rooftop lounge and terrace. A concealed speakeasy tucked within one of the buildings offers a special resident experience. In addition, the development will feature 5,500 square feet of retail space located along South Street. Expected completion is 2028. W.M. Jordan Company will perform construction duties. The post Raleigh Developer to Start Work on 269-Unit Rental Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Blue Heron Asset Management starting 269-unit 'Rosewood' in Raleigh; $12.85M land acquisition
Project completion 2028; 399K SF across two buildings; units 499–1,531 SF; 5.5K SF retail
Premium amenities: sauna, cold plunge, coworking, rooftop lounge, speakeasy; W.M. Jordan construction
stories.summary: Blue Heron Asset Management is beginning construction on Rosewood, a 269-unit apartment community in downtown Raleigh on West South Street, following a $12.85 million land purchase. The mixed-use project spans 399,000 square feet with one- and two-bedroom units, premium resident amenities, and ground-floor retail. Completion is slated for 2028.
stories.impact: Downtown Raleigh's apartment supply is tightening; Rosewood targets affluent renters with lifestyle/wellness amenities (sauna, cold plunge) and mixed-use retail integration, signaling developer confidence in demand for premium rental product in the CBD. The 2028 delivery coincides with Raleigh's continued tech-sector job growth; expect competitive pressure on older stock and upward rent growth for comparable Class A units in the interim, particularly South Raleigh submarkets.

stories.title: Self-Storage Developer Closes On Loan for 1,500-Unit Expansion
stories.excerpt: UTEX Storage Partners closed on a $42.5 million construction loan to finance a two-property self-storage portfolio in the Miami metropolitan area. Affinius Capital and Axonic Capital originated the loan. The financing was arranged by Daniel Hartnett of Greysteel. The first property, located in Pembroke Pines, FL, is a to-be-built, four-story facility which will offer 1,097 units. The second, located in Coral Gables, FL, involves the 540-unit expansion of an existing self-storage facility, which will total 1,500 units upon completion.  Upon completion, the portfolio will total 2,597 units across two properties: a four-story, 100,125-square-foot facility with 1,097 units in Pembroke Pines; and two three-story buildings totaling 132,995 square feet and 1,500 units in Coral Gables. All spaces will be fully climate-controlled with interior and drive-up access and modern loading features for enhanced accessibility. The post Self-Storage Developer Closes On Loan for 1,500-Unit Expansion appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: UTEX Storage Partners closed $42.5M construction loan for 1,500-unit self-storage portfolio, Miami metro
Pembroke Pines: 1,097 units in new 4-story facility; Coral Gables: 540-unit expansion (total 1,500)
Affinius Capital, Axonic Capital originated loan; fully climate-controlled with interior/drive-up access
stories.summary: UTEX Storage Partners secured $42.5 million in construction financing from Affinius Capital and Axonic Capital to expand a two-property self-storage portfolio in the Miami metropolitan area. The Pembroke Pines property will deliver 1,097 new units in a four-story facility, while Coral Gables will add 540 units to an existing facility, totaling 1,500 units across both properties upon completion.
stories.impact: Self-storage supply expansion in Broward and Miami-Dade indicates developer confidence in demand drivers (population inflow, household formation). While multifamily-specific, note that strong self-storage financing activity and cap-rate compression in the category can signal capital competition for yield; however, absorption of storage supply is independent of apartment demand. Limited direct implication for multifamily unless this signals broader logistics/supply-chain consolidation fueling in-migration to South Florida.

stories.title: Berkadia Arranges Sale, Financing of 270-Unit VA Multifamily Property
stories.excerpt: Berkadia has arranged the sale and financing of Masons Keepe, a 270-unit garden-style multifamily community located in Manassas, Virginia.Senior Managing Director Brian Crivella, Managing Directors Bill Gribbin and Yalda Ghamarian, and Associate Director Jack Canepa of Berkadia DC Metro, along with Senior Managing Director Drew White of Berkadia Richmond, arranged the sale on behalf of University Village Apartments, LLC. Senior Managing Director Brad Williamson of Berkadia Miami and Senior Managing Director Christopher Ellis of Berkadia Denver secured financing on behalf of the buyer, Bridge Investment Group. Berkadia originated, and Freddie Mac secured, the five-year, fixed-rate loan with a three-year interest-only period, a 35-year amortization schedule, and a 70% loan-to-value ratio.Located at 9000 Landings Drive, Masons Keepe was built in 2004 and features a mix of one- and two-bedroom apartment homes. The property provides convenient access to employment centers throughout Prince William County and the greater Washington, D.C. metropolitan area. If you’re reading about the multifamily industry, you should be in the room with its leaders. Connect Apartments brings together the executives featured in Connect CRE’s coverage for an afternoon of high-level insights and unmatched networking on September 22 at The Biltmore in LA. The post Berkadia Arranges Sale, Financing of 270-Unit VA Multifamily Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Bridge Investment Group acquired 270-unit Masons Keepe, Manassas, VA; Freddie Mac fixed-rate financing
Property built 2004; one/two-bedroom units; 70% LTV; 5-year fixed, 3-year IO, 35-yr amort.
Located in Prince William County; provides access to DC metro employment centers
stories.summary: Berkadia arranged the sale and acquisition financing of Masons Keepe, a 270-unit garden-style community in Manassas, Virginia, by Bridge Investment Group. The seller was University Village Apartments. Freddie Mac provided a five-year fixed-rate loan with a 70% LTV, three-year interest-only period, and 35-year amortization.
stories.impact: Manassas/Prince William County represents suburban DC exurban growth; Bridge's acquisition signals sustained investor appetite for workforce/middle-market apartments proximate to Northern Virginia employment. Freddie Mac pricing and term reflect stable multifamily credit conditions. Monitor Prince William County job growth and federal workforce expansion (OPM, USAMRIID, etc.) as catalysts for continued rent growth and investor interest; new supply should be underwritten against this employer base.

stories.title: HarbourVest Will Open First NYC Office at 9 West 57th
stories.excerpt: JLL represented global private markets investment firm HarbourVest Partners in securing its first New York City office at Soloviev Group’s 9 W. 57th St. The 10-year lease is for 19,752 square feet on a portion of the building’s 49th floor. Headquartered in Boston, HarbourVest is among the world’s leading private markets investment firms, managing more than $160 billion in assets. The firm’s New York office marks a milestone in its continued growth. JLL vice chairman Alexander Chudnoff and associate Ava Loughlin represented HarbourVest. Ownership was represented by CBRE’s Howard Fiddle, John Maher, Gregg Rothkin and Alex Leopold. “HarbourVest wanted its office in New York City to reflect the caliber of its business, and to provide an exceptional experience for both clients and employees,” said Chudnoff. “Few buildings deliver that combination better than 9 W. 57th St., which continues to attract many of the world’s most prestigious financial and investment firms.” The post HarbourVest Will Open First NYC Office at 9 West 57th appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: HarbourVest Partners signed 10-year, 19.75K SF lease at 9 W. 57th St., NYC (49th floor)
Boston-based firm managing $160B+ in assets opening first NYC office; 10-year commitment
Soloviev Group ownership; JLL represents institutional capital migration to Manhattan
stories.summary: HarbourVest Partners, a Boston-based private markets investment firm managing over $160 billion in assets, leased 19,752 square feet on the 49th floor of 9 W. 57th St. in Manhattan for ten years, marking the firm's first New York City office. JLL represented HarbourVest in the transaction.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily relevance. However, capital firm relocations and office expansions to NYC signal confidence in the market's institutional ecosystem and talent attraction. Indirect signal: if PE/VC capital is accumulating in Manhattan, it may increase M&A pressure on stabilized multifamily assets and heighten acquisition competition in nearby multifamily markets (Brooklyn, Jersey City); monitor for portfolio sales of older garden-style buildings.

stories.title: Retail Veterans Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy Join JLL
stories.excerpt: There are some new recruits at JLL, the brokerage firm announced Monday. Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy, who co-founded retail and entertainment real estate firm Dreamscape, have joined JLL as executive managing director and senior vice president, respectively, to help further drive the firm’s experiential retail, entertainment and mixed-use leasing initiatives. “Josh and Scott have built exceptional careers helping owners and brands create compelling destinations that resonate with today’s consumers,” Patrick A. Smith, vice chairman at JLL, said in a statement. “Their relationships, creativity and deep understanding of the retail landscape will be a tremendous asset to our clients and further enhance the capabilities of both our New York and national project leasing platforms.” The pair have advised on some of the most notable mixed-use and entertainment leases in the country over the last 10 years, including deals at the South Street Seaport in New York City, retail marketplace the Arcade in Nashville, and shopping center Bayside Marketplace in Miami. “Scott and I are excited to join JLL at a time when retail continues to evolve across both urban and suburban markets,” Strauss said in a statement. “While experiential retail has become an increasingly important driver of consumer demand, we remain deeply focused on helping landlords and tenants navigate every aspect of the retail landscape.” Before joining Dreamscape, Strauss and Zinovoy also worked together at independent real estate firm RKF and Newmark, which acquired RKF in 2018. “We were drawn to JLL because of its industry-leading platform, global reach, outstanding reputation and, most importantly, its people and collaborative culture,” Zinovoy said in a statement. “JLL provides the ideal environment to continue growing our business while helping clients bring transformative visions to life.” Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Joshua Strauss, Scott Zinovoy join JLL as EMD/SVP from Dreamscape retail firm
Retail/entertainment real estate veterans; track record: South Street Seaport NYC, Arcade Nashville, Bayside Miami
Strengthens JLL's experiential retail, entertainment, mixed-use leasing platform nationally
stories.summary: Retail veterans Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy, co-founders of Dreamscape, joined JLL as executive managing director and senior vice president respectively. The pair brings deep expertise in experiential retail and mixed-use development, with notable transactions in South Street Seaport, the Arcade in Nashville, and Bayside Marketplace in Miami.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. Hiring signals continued broker capital investment in entertainment/lifestyle retail integration within mixed-use projects. Relevant for multifamily developers planning ground-floor retail strategy; rising sophistication in experiential retail leasing may increase development costs and entitlement timelines for mixed-use projects but also strengthen NOI via higher-quality tenant curation.

stories.title: Infinium Wall Systems Takes 30K SF of Office, Retail Space at ESRT’s 1359 Broadway
stories.excerpt: If these walls could talk, they’d talk about all the new leasing in Midtown. Infinium Wall Systems, a company that designs, engineers and installs wall systems and doors for offices, has inked a new office and retail lease spanning 30,017 square feet at Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT)’s 1359 Broadway, the landlord announced Monday. “We are pleased to welcome Infinium to 1359 Broadway,” Ryan Kass, executive vice president, co-head of real estate and chief revenue officer at ESRT, said in a statement. “Innovative companies continue to seek well-located, modernized office space in New York City.” The asking rent and the length of the lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for office space in Midtown was $84.99 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers research. Sarah Pontius, Jerica Lam, Carleigh Bettiol and Charlotte Reaman from Artisan Alliance represented Infinium in the deal. Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini, and Kerry Lavelle represented ESRT in-house, along with Robert Lowe, Ron Lo Russo, Anthony LoPresti and Dan Organ from Cushman & Wakefield. Spokespeople for the brokerages did not immediately respond to requests for comment. “We evaluated numerous locations throughout Midtown, and 1359 Broadway consistently stood out,” Shawn Gaffney, founder of Infinium Wall Systems, told Commercial Observer. “Beyond the quality of the building itself, ESRT’s commitment to sustainability, long-term ownership, and partnership aligned closely with our own values. The southern exposure, private entrance, outdoor terrace, and opportunity to create a truly differentiated space made it the clear choice for our New York flagship.” This deal represents an expansion for Infinium, which also has an office and showroom address at 44 East 32nd Street. Built in 1924, 1359 Broadway is a 22-story office property with other tenants such as artificial intelligence company Scaled Cognition and nonprofit Braven. Amanda Schi
bullets: Infinium Wall Systems leased 30K SF office/retail at ESRT's 1359 Broadway, Midtown Manhattan
Midtown average asking rent Q2 2026: $84.99/SF; Infinium terms not disclosed
ESRT filling space with innovative companies; recent office leasing momentum in Midtown
stories.summary: Infinium Wall Systems, a specialized office systems manufacturer, signed a lease for 30,017 square feet at Empire State Realty Trust's 1359 Broadway in Midtown Manhattan. The average asking rent in Midtown was $84.99 per square foot in Q2 2026, though specific lease terms were not disclosed.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. Office leasing activity in Midtown indicates tenant demand and cap-rate stabilization in the sector, but does not signal residential demand or supply signals absent broader employment trends (covered elsewhere).

stories.title: Prologis Promotes Travis Harvey to Global Head of Development
stories.excerpt: Prologis has promoted Travis Harvey to lead the firm’s global development and construction platform across the Americas, Asia and Europe, Commercial Observer has learned. As global head of development, Harvey will use his more than 20 years of experience in the industry — including 11 years at Prologis — to strengthen the industrial giant’s platform. He will replace Greg Bauer, who recently retired from Prologis after 30 years with the company. “I’m grateful for the opportunity to build on the legacy Greg helped shape. Development is central to how we serve our customers, support the communities where we build and deliver value to investors,” Harvey said in a statement. “My focus will be on strengthening our platform, deepening our use of technology and data, and continuing to support the talented teams that bring our projects to life around the world.” Before joining Prologis, Harvey was vice president of land development at Flagler Global Logistics, where he worked on developing “strategic land assets” in Florida, according to Prologis. Replacing Harvey in his old position as senior vice president and head of development management for Prologis’ U.S. East region is Sheila Sutton, who was previously the firm’s vice president and development officer for the New York and New Jersey markets. Sheila Sutton. PHOTO: Courtesy Prologis Sutton joined Prologis in 2022 from Duke Realty, where she was vice president of construction operations and managed multiple projects throughout New Jersey and Pennsylvania. Prologis acquired Duke Realty in October 2022. “Prologis has established a track record in the East Region for delivering innovative, high-quality logistics projects in some of the most important hubs in the global supply chain, from Toronto down to Miami,” Sutton said in a statement. “I’m excited to build on that momentum with our teams, customers and local partners as we continue to deliver projects that support local economies and communities.” Both Harvey and Sutton
bullets: Prologis promotes Travis Harvey to global head of development across Americas, Asia, Europe
Harvey replacing retiring Greg Bauer (30 years); 20+ years industry experience, 11 years at Prologis
Focus: strengthen platform, deepen technology/data use, support teams globally
stories.summary: Prologis promoted Travis Harvey to global head of development, replacing retiring Greg Bauer. Harvey brings over 20 years of industry experience, including 11 years at Prologis, and previously held a land development role at Flagler Global Logistics in Florida.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. Industrial REIT leadership changes are tangential unless they signal major portfolio shifts or capital reallocation away from logistics toward residential; no evidence here. Monitor Prologis' capital allocation strategy for any residential-adjacent developments.

stories.title: Infinity, KB Realty Partners Bag 75 Kenmare Street’s Retail Condo for $12M
stories.excerpt: A joint venture went shopping near SoHo and didn’t come away empty-handed. Infinity Real Estate and KB Realty Partners just acquired the retail condominium at 75 Kenmare Street in Manhattan’s Nolita neighborhood—just outside of SoHo — for $11.75 million, Commercial Observer can first report. The transaction, which closed July 16, adds to Infinity’s continued push into SoHo retail, and KB Realty’s expanding portfolio in the neighborhood via both retail and mixed-use investments. 75 Kenmare Street sits in a prime position at the corner of Kenmare and Mulberry streets. Its 10,000-square-foot retail component comprises two commercial units that are fully leased to tenants Undefeated, Nemesis Coffee, Bondi Sushi and M Jewelers. Atop the retail sits a luxury condo component — not included in the purchase — with 38 residential units designed by none other than Lenny Kravitz. First Atlantic Realty was the seller, and was advised by Masonre in the deal. ConnectOne Bank provided Infinity and KB Realty with acquisition financing. “Manhattan retail is where Infinity got its start nearly 20 years ago, and this acquisition marks a deliberate return to that core focus,” Steven Kassin, a managing partner at Infinity Real Estate, said in a statement. “75 Kenmare Street is an attractive asset to continue growing our footprint in Manhattan retail.” Kassin founded Infinity Real Estate in 2005. The firm — which develops, owns and manages real estate across the U.S. — is headquartered in New York, but also has an office in Miami today. “This is exactly the kind of asset we look for,” David Berg, a partner at Infinity Real Estate, said. “Strong in-place tenancy, dependable cash flow from day one, and a tenant mix that genuinely fits the neighborhood.” The deal follows Infinity Real Estate’s acquisition of 40 Bleecker Street in late 2024. The firm paid $13.3 million to Broad Street Development and Crow Holdings for the similarly sized retail condominium, also
bullets: Infinity Real Estate, KB Realty Partners acquired 75 Kenmare retail condo, SoHo/Nolita, for $11.75M
10K SF retail, fully leased: Undefeated, Nemesis Coffee, Bondi Sushi, M Jewelers; 38 condo units above
Corner Kenmare/Mulberry; First Atlantic Realty seller; July 2026 close
stories.summary: A joint venture of Infinity Real Estate and KB Realty Partners acquired the retail condominium at 75 Kenmare Street in Manhattan's Nolita neighborhood for $11.75 million. The property comprises 10,000 square feet of fully-leased retail (four tenants) and 38 luxury condo units above, designed by Lenny Kravitz.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance; this is a trophy retail asset acquisition. However, strong pricing for small retail condos in SoHo/Nolita reflects sustained luxury consumer spending and tourism-dependent retail confidence in the market. May indirectly support rental demand for high-income households nearby, particularly in adjacent Nolita/Tribeca rental corridors.

stories.title: Law Firm Hodgson Russ Renews 20K SF at Fisher Brothers’ 605 Third Avenue
stories.excerpt: Law firm Hodgson Russ spearheaded a rush of new leases at Fisher Brothers’ 605 Third Avenue. The firm renewed its 19,900-square-foot lease on the 23rd floor of the tower, located between East 39th and East 40th streets, in June, according to the landlord. Newmark’s Brian Cohen and Kyle Ciminelli negotiated the 10-year renewal on behalf of the law firm. Several deals inked in the building since April secured rents up to $120 per square foot, according to Fisher Brothers. The landlord, represented in-house by Clark Briffel, did not disclose exact prices. Other recent activity in the 44-story building included private equity firm Coalesce Capital’s 16,900-square-foot renewal and expansion in June. The deal secured Coalesce its home on the 36th floor for the next five years. Meanwhile, investment firm Lionheart Strategic Management also expanded in June, taking 6,400 additional square feet on the 16th floor for 10 years. Lionheart’s total footprint at the property following the new expansion was unclear. Both firms were represented in-house in the lease deals. These busy tenants can expect to remain caffeinated for the next decade, at least, as ground-floor retail tenant Starbucks signed a 10-year renewal across 1,800 square feet in July. David Firestein of TSCG negotiated on behalf of the coffee chain. A 130,000-square-foot block of availability on 605 Third Avenue’s fourth, fifth and sixth floors remains, according to the Fisher Brothers. The landlord has retained a CBRE team of Howard Fiddle, Peter Turchin, Ben Joseph, Brett Shannon, Max Tarter, Zachary Price and Natalie Chung to market the spaces. Spokespeople for Newmark and the tenants did not immediately respond to requests for comment. TSCG could not be reached for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Hodgson Russ renewed 19.9K SF at Fisher Brothers' 605 Third Avenue, Midtown, 10-year term
Recent leases at 605 Third: up to $120/SF; Coalesce Capital 16.9K SF renewal/expansion, Lionheart 6.4K SF expansion
44-story tower between East 39th-40th; recent office leasing momentum
stories.summary: Law firm Hodgson Russ renewed its 19,900-square-foot lease on the 23rd floor of 605 Third Avenue for ten years. Recent leasing activity at the building achieved rents up to $120 per square foot, with other tenants including Coalesce Capital and Lionheart Strategic Management also renewing and expanding.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. Office demand signals in Midtown may support adjacent residential neighborhoods if tied to broader employment gains, but alone do not move multifamily development metrics.

stories.title: Stetson University plans Tampa development as higher ed bets on mixed-use districts
stories.excerpt: Stetson University is joining the higher education trend of investing in mixed-use districts, with the redevelopment of its Tampa Law Center. Stetson is partnering with New York-based Bromley Companies on plans to develop 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 square feet of retail space and 440,000 square feet of office space, along with a new law center, on the 6.5-acre site in the Tampa Heights district, according to ConnectCRE.  Stetson University acquired the property from the City of Tampa in 2002, according to the Tampa Business Journal. It was previously the site of the Tampa Heights police station.  The university’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Stetson University, Bromley Companies plan 6.5-acre Tampa Heights mixed-use: 670 apts, 300 hotel keys, 37.5K SF retail
440K SF office, new law school; 1,000 students enrolled across Stetson law campuses; construction late 2026
Rezoning required; modeled on USF Fletcher District ($268M, 27-acre); phased development
stories.summary: Stetson University partnered with New York-based Bromley Companies to redevelop its 6.5-acre Tampa Law Center site into a mixed-use district comprising 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 square feet of retail, 440,000 square feet of office space, and a new law school. The project, which requires Tampa City Council rezoning approval, is expected to begin construction in late 2026 and will be delivered in three phases.
stories.impact: Major signal for Tampa multifamily supply pipeline and demand drivers. Stetson's bet on mixed-use (following USF's Fletcher District model) reflects institutional confidence in Tampa's student population, housing demand, and urban revitalization. The 670-unit apartment delivery (likely student-focused initially, eventual stabilized rental) adds meaningful new supply to Tampa Heights; monitor absorption and rent rates for competitive pressure on adjacent student and young professional markets. Law school expansion signals sustained enrollment; project phasing over 3+ years should allow market absorption without disruption.

stories.title: Fraud scheme: real estate journalism’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads”
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads” usually includes some version of the phrase “fraud scheme.” DFW real estate’s latest 15-car pileup on I-35 is the case of two Frisco entrepreneurs who already had a go-round with allegations of a “Ponzi scheme” on their track record before concocting promotional materials for a luxury waterfront development on the San Gabriel River that they never intended to build, the Dallas Morning News reported. Promotional materials for the 400-acre development showed a mixed-use development with office retail, restaurants, parks and a canal big enough for boats on the Central Texas river […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan, Gounder liable for fraud; $400M damages (incl. $300M punitive)
Fake San Gabriel River mixed-use development (400 acres, Dripping Springs); swindled $21M infrastructure work
Pair previously faced SEC Ponzi scheme allegations (2023); accused of defrauding DFW Indian-American community $130M
stories.summary: A Williamson County jury found Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for breach of contract and fraud, ordering them to pay $400 million in damages to Energy Commissioning Inc., a Georgetown firm they hired for $21 million in infrastructure work on a fake 400-acre mixed-use development on the San Gabriel River. The pair was previously targeted by the SEC in 2023 for an alleged Ponzi scheme defrauding the DFW Indian-American community of $130 million.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to mainstream multifamily development. This is a cautionary tale on due diligence and fraud risk in Texas real estate; may indicate elevated regulatory scrutiny of DFW development partnerships and minority-community investments post-2023 SEC action. No implications for supply, demand, capital, or costs unless broader lending/capital verification tightens for DFW developers.

stories.title: Align ups unit count, lowers height for controversial Marina Safeway project
stories.excerpt: Align Real Estate is making its contentious Marina Safeway redevelopment a little shorter and a little denser, revising its proposal as it tries to steer one of San Francisco’s most closely watched housing projects through city approval. The San Francisco-based developer submitted updated plans that would reduce the project’s tallest towers from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories while increasing the residential count from 790 to 848 units, the San Francisco Chronicle reported. The redesign trims roughly 40 feet from the east tower and 23 feet from the west tower with the entire development at 15 Marina […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Align Real Estate revising Marina Safeway redevelopment: 848 units (↑58), reduced height (25→22, 22→18 stories)
989.5K SF mixed-use at 15 Marina Boulevard, SF waterfront; reduces amenity space, trims nonresidential
Mayor Lurie still opposes; AB 2011 streamlined approval process; Planning Dept. review ongoing
stories.summary: Align Real Estate submitted revised plans for the contentious Marina Safeway redevelopment in San Francisco, increasing residential units from 790 to 848 while reducing the tallest towers from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories. The 989,500-square-foot project on the SF waterfront achieves density gains by optimizing floor plans, reducing amenity space, and trimming ground-floor nonresidential uses. The project remains under review under Assembly Bill 2011, though Mayor Daniel Lurie has not endorsed the revised proposal.
stories.impact: Critical signal on San Francisco apartment supply and entitlement environment. Align's yield optimization (more units, less floor area) reflects cost pressures and competitive underwriting; height concessions indicate political constraints limiting upside density. AB 2011 process creates faster approval path but doesn't guarantee political support. If Marina Safeway (a trophy waterfront project) faces sustained mayoral opposition, broader SF entitlement timeline and political risk increase. Monitor: project approval likelihood, comparable tower pricing in SF (condo vs. rental), and implications for Marina District rent growth once delivered (likely 2028+).

stories.title: Miami design board swoons over OKO Lilli, 13th Floor’s Nobu condo towers
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s second condo tower in Edgewater and 13th Floor Investments’ proposal for a Nobu-branded residential building next to a historic Brickell church are advancing.  The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of both developments on Wednesday, with most board members gushing over the designs of what would be the latest luxury tower additions to the city’s quickly evolving skyline. One board member voted no on Doronin’s planned building, citing issues with some of the design.  Miami-based OKO Group, led by Doronin, plans the 53-story, 117-unit project, called OKO Lilli Tower, on the half-acre lot at 717 Northeast […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami UDRB approved OKO Lilli (Doronin): 53-story, 117-unit tower at 717 NE 27th, Edgewater
13th Floor/Nobu 619 Brickell: 75-story, 321-unit tower at 619 Brickell Ave.; 2-acre parcel near church
Both developments cleared design review; final approval with planning/zoning director
stories.summary: The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of two luxury residential towers: OKO Group's 53-story, 117-unit OKO Lilli Tower in Edgewater (with 244-space parking garage and ground-floor retail), and 13th Floor Investments' 75-story, 321-unit Nobu-branded tower at 619 Brickell Avenue adjacent to First Miami Presbyterian Church. Both projects advanced design review, though final city approval remains pending.
stories.impact: Miami luxury condo supply pipeline tightening in Edgewater and Brickell; both projects signal sustained institutional confidence in high-end residential demand despite recent condo market volatility. UDRB approval de-risks entitlement. Monitor: Nobu's 321-unit delivery (likely 2029+) as a bellwether for rental conversion risk in Miami's luxury segment. Combined 438 units in flagship neighborhoods may apply competitive pressure to existing Class A rental stock; if strong unit absorption occurs, implies healthy underlying demand and possible rent growth for mid-market (non-luxury) multifamily nearby (Wynwood, Design District).

stories.title: Palm Beach County nixes proposed Project Tango data center
stories.excerpt: Plans for a proposed data center in Palm Beach County hit a major roadblock Wednesday after county commissioners rejected a zoning application that would have allowed the project’s development.  At the end of a 12-hour hearing, officials voted 5-1 to deny an amended zoning application that would have permitted for a 1 million-square-foot data center at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The project also would have included 2.3 million square feet of warehouses and 216,000 square feet for minor utility use.  Plans for Central Park Commerce Center, also known as Project Tango, were filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Palm Beach County commissioners rejected zoning for Project Tango: 1M SF data center, 2.3M SF warehouse
20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard; PBA Holdings applicant; 130 acres of ~200-acre parcel
Vote 5-1 denial; community pushback on infrastructure/environmental impact; motion without prejudice
stories.summary: Palm Beach County commissioners voted 5-1 to deny an amended zoning application for Project Tango (Central Park Commerce Center), a proposed 1 million-square-foot data center and 2.3 million square feet of warehouses at State Road 80/Southern Boulevard. The rejection followed a 12-hour hearing with over 80 public speakers opposing the project due to concerns about infrastructure and community impacts. The motion was made without prejudice, allowing resubmission.
stories.impact: Reflects Palm Beach County's regulatory resistance to large-scale logistics/data center development. The rejection reduces industrial supply growth in a supply-constrained county; limited direct multifamily impact unless industrial job creation was expected to drive population and rental demand in the region. Developers should note heightened political/community scrutiny of major industrial projects in Palm Beach; alternative industrial sites (Broward, St. Lucie) may see increased activity and higher land costs.

stories.title: Musk’s companies prep $5M Seaholm office renovation
stories.excerpt: Downtown Austin’s Seaholm Power Plant, which was quietly occupied by several Elon Musk-affiliated companies earlier this year, is getting a $5.2 million facelift.  The planned renovations have been put in place by Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, the firm that is pushing the construction of the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County. Musk’s companies occupy 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, according to the Austin American Statesman. Musk is typically bombastic about his companies’ pursuits and ambitious, but has not yet publicly commented on the office space. He has given notes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elon Musk-affiliated companies prepping $5.2M renovation at Seaholm Power Plant, Austin; 35K SF occupied
Wit Tech (SpaceX link) driving renovations; 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, Downtown Austin
Musk's Terafab semiconductor plant (Grimes County): $55B project, 6K+ acres, tax incentives approved; 100M SF potential
stories.summary: Musk-affiliated companies occupying 35,000 square feet at the Seaholm Power Plant in downtown Austin are undertaking a $5.2 million renovation. The renovation is being orchestrated by Wit Tech, which has ties to SpaceX. Separately, Musk is advancing the $55 billion Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County, which has already secured tax breaks and a tax increment reinvestment zone approval.
stories.impact: Major supply-side and demand signal for Central Texas multifamily. Terafab's $55B investment and potential 100M SF footprint represents transformational job creation and population inflow to the Austin metro and Grimes County (rural north of Austin). Expect sustained migration of tech/manufacturing workers, wage growth, and sustained rental demand across Austin submarkets over 5–10 years. However, the Grimes County site is exurban (40+ miles north); multifamily developers should focus on in-fill Austin (Downtown, East Austin, North Austin near I-35) and I-35 corridor submarkets (Pflugerville, Leander) as primary beneficiaries of employment spillover. Monitor infrastructure (water, power, transport) and housing feasibility in Grimes County itself; Musk's "forgiveness not permission" ethos suggests aggressive timelines and cost-cutting that may pressure construction labor and materials in Central Texas. Tax incentives to Terafab may create precedent for workforce housing/multifamily incentives.
